symbols: limp body of Christ — total self-emptying (kenosis) and the reality of the Incarnate God's true death
stone slab — the altar of sacrifice prefiguring the Eucharistic table
mourning figures — the Church grieving at the foot of the cross, the community of faith at the threshold of Holy Saturday
dramatic clouds — cosmic disturbance at the death of the Creator, echoing the darkness over the land
outstretched arms of Christ — the completed sacrifice, arms that embraced all humanity on the cross now surrendered in death
burial linens — preparation for entombment fulfilling prophecy, prefiguring the grave cloths found empty at the Resurrection
description: A dramatic drawing depicting the dead body of Christ being laid upon a stone slab. The lifeless figure lies horizontally, pale and limp, with arms extended. Several mourning figures surround the body: a robed figure at left gestures upward in grief, while others cluster behind, bending toward the body with expressions of sorrow. Turbulent, swirling clouds dominate the upper portion of the composition. The scene conveys profound weight and desolation, with strong contrasts between light and shadow across the figures and landscape.
theological_description: The Deposition represents the pivotal threshold between the completed sacrifice of Calvary and the silence of Holy Saturday. Theologically, it proclaims the full reality of Christ's death — the Incarnate Word truly died, affirming against Docetism that God assumed human nature even unto death. The Fathers, especially Athanasius and Cyril of Alexandria, emphasized that the Logos did not abandon the body in death but remained hypostatically united to it, sanctifying even the grave. The stone slab prefigures the altar: as the body was laid upon stone, so the Eucharistic sacrifice is offered upon the altar. The mourning figures embody the Church in its darkest hour, the seed buried in the earth (John 12:24) that must die before it bears fruit. Typologically, Christ's three days in the tomb fulfill the sign of Jonah (Matthew 12:40), and as Isaac was received back from the dead in figure (Hebrews 11:19), so Christ will rise. Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus, secret disciples who emerge in Christ's death, represent the courageous faith that blooms precisely when all seems lost — a patristic symbol of the catechumen who dares to approach the cross.

scripture_refs.reason: Direct account of Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus taking the body of Jesus, wrapping it in linen with spices, and laying it in a new tomb
Matthew's account of Joseph of Arimathea requesting the body from Pilate, wrapping it in clean linen, and placing it in his own new tomb
Mark's account of the burial, noting Joseph's boldness in going to Pilate and the women observing where Jesus was laid
Luke's account of the burial and the women preparing spices and ointments before resting on the Sabbath
Prophecy that the Suffering Servant would be assigned a grave with the rich in his death, fulfilled by burial in Joseph's tomb
The man of sorrows, acquainted with grief, wounded for our transgressions — the theological backdrop of the entire Deposition scene
Messianic psalm describing the physical suffering and desolation of the crucified one whose body is now being laid to rest
The sign of Jonah — as Jonah was three days in the belly of the great fish, so the Son of Man will be three days in the heart of the earth
Unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit — the burial as the planting of redemption
Abraham received Isaac back from the dead in figure, typologically prefiguring the burial and resurrection of Christ
The binding of Isaac as a type of Christ's sacrificial death; Isaac carried the wood as Christ carried the cross
Jonah in the belly of the fish for three days prefigures Christ's three days in the tomb
You will not abandon my soul to Sheol or let your Holy One see corruption — the promise underlying the burial scene that death will not have the final word
Buried with Christ in baptism — the Deposition is the sacramental archetype of Christian burial into Christ's death
We were buried with him through baptism into death, so that as Christ was raised, we too may walk in newness of life
